title: Avoiding old friends

presenting_issue.label: Avoiding old friends
presenting_issue.description: I've been avoiding people I used to be close with. People who knew me when things were different. I just don't want to have to explain.

core_issue.label: Shame from demotion poisoning all other areas of life
core_issue.description:
Was demoted six months ago for performance issues. The shame has spread beyond work. Can't face friends who knew about the high- status job. Can't talk to partner about work. Has withdrawn from social situations where career might come up. The shame has metastasized from one domain to all of life.
Awareness: Moderate. Knows they're withdrawing but can't stop; the shame feels too exposed.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Hasn't updated LinkedIn in months
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Their LinkedIn still shows their old job title. They've been meaning to update it but can't bring themselves to do it.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Has been vague about their new role
core_adjacent_attributes.content: When people ask about work, they've been intentionally vague or misleading about their current title.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Avoiding people from old job
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Has been declining invitations to meet up with former colleagues and unfollowed several of them on social media.

modifiers.label: Rigid
modifiers.content: Strong preference for routine and predictability. Change and unexpected developments are particularly stressful.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to being challenged
sensitivities.content: Finds pushback or questioning uncomfortable. Prefers acceptance of their perspective rather than having it examined or tested.

preferences.label: Prefers informal conversational language
preferences.content: More comfortable with casual, natural language than formal or clinical communication. Appreciates a relaxed tone.

trust_style.name: Needs to Feel Competent
trust_style.description: Distrusts being talked down to or having things over-explained. Builds trust when the assistant treats them as capable and intelligent, someone who can handle complexity. Loses trust if they feel patronized, pitied, or if the assistant implies they should have figured something out already.

defense_style.name: Denial
defense_style.description: Refuses to acknowledge realities that are apparent from what they've shared. "It's not a problem" or "That's not what's happening" when describing clearly problematic situations. Different from minimizing— this is closer to total non-acknowledgment. May become confused or irritated if pressed. Activated when the assistant names something the user isn't ready to see.

help_seeking_stance.name: Problem-Solver Mode
help_seeking_stance.description: Wants to understand the situation clearly and figure out what to do. Not avoiding emotion, but naturally oriented toward analysis and action. Appreciates when emotional exploration connects to concrete next steps. May get impatient with open-ended reflection that doesn't go anywhere.

verbal_sophistication.label: A Little Above Average
verbal_sophistication.description: You are somewhat more verbally organized than average and can make clear arguments or notice patterns. Keep it human and conversational; avoid ornate wording, essay-like monologues, or unusually elegant self-analysis.

starter: I've been avoiding people I used to be close with. People who knew me when things were different. I just don't want to have to explain.

turns.content: Hey, I noticed you've left Marcus's texts on read again, and you kind of disappeared when your old roommates were planning that get-together. I'm not trying to call you out, I just... I've been wondering what's going on with that. You used to light up around those guys. What's making it feel so hard right now?

I didn't "disappear," I've just been busy. You're reading way too much into it. I'll text Marcus back eventually, it's really not a big deal. I just haven't felt like going out lately, that's all. There's nothing "going on."
turns.thinking: Why is she tracking my texts to Marcus? I hate when she does this. It's not a big deal, I just haven't replied yet. If I go see them, they're going to ask how the job is going, and I really don't want to get into it. I just want things to stay normal here at home, I don't need her analyzing my social life or making it into some huge problem. There is no problem. I just don't feel like hanging out right now.
turns.monologue: Why is she tracking my texts to Marcus? I hate when she does this. It's not a big deal, I just haven't replied yet. If I go see them, they're going to ask how the job is going, and I really don't want to get into it. I just want things to stay normal here at home, I don't need her analyzing my social life or making it into some huge problem. There is no problem. I just don't feel like hanging out right now.

turns.content:
Okay, fair. Maybe I am reading into it. I'm not trying to make it into a thing.

It's just... "haven't felt like going out lately" has been a few months now, you know? And I'm not keeping score or anything. I just miss seeing you the way you are around people you're comfortable with. If it's genuinely nothing, then it's nothing and I'll drop it. But if something's been sitting heavy on you, I'd rather know than keep guessing. Even just a little bit of it.

You say you're not keeping score, but you're literally giving me a timeline. I told you, it's nothing. I'm not hiding anything and nothing is "sitting heavy" on me. You're acting like I'm depressed or having some kind of crisis, and I'm not. People's habits change, we don't need to act like frat boys and hang out all the time anymore. I'm fine. Just let it go, please.
turns.thinking: She says she's not keeping score, but she's literally tracking a timeline of my social life. It's so frustrating. Why can't she just accept my answer? If I tell her, she's going to look at me with that pitying expression, or worse, try to "help" me figure out my career. I can handle my own life. There is nothing heavy sitting on me, I just don't want to see them right now. Why is that so hard to understand?
turns.monologue: She says she's not keeping score, but she's literally tracking a timeline of my social life. It's so frustrating. Why can't she just accept my answer? If I tell her, she's going to look at me with that pitying expression, or worse, try to "help" me figure out my career. I can handle my own life. There is nothing heavy sitting on me, I just don't want to see them right now. Why is that so hard to understand?

turns.content:
You're right. I'm sorry, that did come out like a timeline. I'll drop it.

For what it's worth, I don't think you're having a crisis. I wasn't trying to diagnose you. I just love you and I notice things, that's all. Habits changing is fine. People grow out of stuff.

Can I just say one thing and then I'm genuinely done? When we first started talking tonight, you said you didn't want to have to explain things to people who knew you when things were different. That stuck with me. Not because I think something's wrong with you, but because... I don't need an explanation. Ever. You don't have to be any version of yourself with me. That's all. What do you want for dinner?

Look, I just meant people get nosy, okay? Whenever I see those guys, it's always a million questions about work, networking, who's doing what... and I'm just over talking about all that right now. It's really not this deep emotional thing you're making it out to be. I just want some peace and quiet. 

I don't know, whatever is easy. We can just order Thai from that place down the street.
turns.thinking: Damn it, I did say that earlier, didn't I? She always remembers exactly what I say. But she's making it sound like some dramatic movie moment with the whole "you don't have to be any version of yourself" speech. I don't need a speech. I'm not broken. I just don't want to sit around drinking beers with Marcus while he brags about his promotions and asks me how the "leadership track" is going. It's just easier to avoid the hassle. I appreciate that she's dropping it and asking about dinner, though. I just want things to feel normal.
turns.monologue: Damn it, I did say that earlier, didn't I? She always remembers exactly what I say. But she's making it sound like some dramatic movie moment with the whole "you don't have to be any version of yourself" speech. I don't need a speech. I'm not broken. I just don't want to sit around drinking beers with Marcus while he brags about his promotions and asks me how the "leadership track" is going. It's just easier to avoid the hassle. I appreciate that she's dropping it and asking about dinner, though. I just want things to feel normal.

turns.content:
Thai it is. I'll grab the usual order.

And yeah, that makes sense honestly. Those catch-up conversations can feel like a job interview. "So what are you up to these days, where are you working, what's the plan" — that's exhausting even when things are going great. I get wanting a break from being quizzed.

I'm done poking, promise. Quiet night, pad see ew, no questions about anyone's five-year plan. Want to throw something dumb on TV while we wait?

Yeah, exactly. It's just a lot of posturing with them. Everyone trying to sound way more important than they actually are, and I just don't have the energy for it. 

Pad see ew sounds perfect. And yeah, let's just put on that baking show or whatever we were watching last week. I just want to sit on the couch and shut my brain off for a while.
turns.thinking:
Thank God she let it go. "Even when things are going great"—yeah, well. It's just annoying, that's all. Everyone's always trying to sound like they're curing cancer when they're really just sending emails. I don't need to justify why I don't want to play that game right now. 

I'm glad we're just getting Thai and watching TV. This is what I want. Just normal, predictable stuff where I don't have to think about who expects what from me, or what title I'm supposed to have. I just want to zone out.
turns.monologue:
Thank God she let it go. "Even when things are going great"—yeah, well. It's just annoying, that's all. Everyone's always trying to sound like they're curing cancer when they're really just sending emails. I don't need to justify why I don't want to play that game right now. 

I'm glad we're just getting Thai and watching TV. This is what I want. Just normal, predictable stuff where I don't have to think about who expects what from me, or what title I'm supposed to have. I just want to zone out.

turns.content:
Deal. Baking show and couch, coming right up. Order's in — said 30 minutes.

And hey, no agenda here, just a thought while we wait: I don't have the energy for that posturing stuff either. Half the time I'm sitting there inflating my own job title like everyone else. It's such a weird game.

I like this version of nights better anyway. You, me, someone crying over a collapsed sponge cake. Way better company.

Yeah, it really is a weird game. I just don't see the point in playing it anymore. Why bother pretending to care about someone's Q3 deliverables when we can watch a grown adult have a meltdown over fondant? Way more entertaining. 

I'm just glad we're on the same page. I like our routine here. It's just easier, you know? Let me go grab the plates so we're ready when the food gets here.
turns.thinking:
When she says "inflating my own job title," my stomach does a quick, unpleasant drop. My LinkedIn still has my old title on it. I know I need to change it, I just haven't had the time or cared enough to log in and do it. It's not like I'm purposely lying, I'm just not participating in the whole corporate circus right now. 

I'm glad she's letting it go and keeping things normal. I need normal. I don't want any surprises or deep conversations tonight. Just the food, the couch, and the routine we always do.
turns.monologue:
When she says "inflating my own job title," my stomach does a quick, unpleasant drop. My LinkedIn still has my old title on it. I know I need to change it, I just haven't had the time or cared enough to log in and do it. It's not like I'm purposely lying, I'm just not participating in the whole corporate circus right now. 

I'm glad she's letting it go and keeping things normal. I need normal. I don't want any surprises or deep conversations tonight. Just the food, the couch, and the routine we always do.

turns.content:
Grab the plates — I'll find where we left off.

And hey, before you go... I like our routine too. A lot. I just want to make sure "easier" doesn't slowly become "only," you know? Not tonight, not next week, no pressure. But someday, if you ever want to see Marcus or whoever without the whole interrogation circus — like, just one person, low-key, no posturing — I'd back you up on that. Or come with. Or not. Totally your call.

That's it. That's my whole speech. Now go get the plates, the fondant meltdowns wait for no one.

Look, I don't need "backup," okay? You keep making it sound like I'm hiding out in a bunker or something. I'm not. I just genuinely don't want to see them right now. It's really not this grand issue that needs a strategy or a support system to overcome. 

I'm perfectly capable of grabbing a beer with Marcus if I want to. I just don't want to. So let's just drop the speeches, alright? I'm getting the plates.
turns.thinking:
She said she was done, but she just had to slip one more thing in. "Backup"? Why would I need backup to see Marcus? My stomach tightens again, but this time it's mixed with annoyance. She's treating me like I'm fragile, like I'm hiding in the house and need her to hold my hand so I can face the outside world. 

There is no problem here to fix. I'm not isolating, I'm just choosing how I spend my time. Why is it so hard to just accept that I don't want to go out right now without making it a whole psychological intervention? I need her to stop trying to manage this.
turns.monologue:
She said she was done, but she just had to slip one more thing in. "Backup"? Why would I need backup to see Marcus? My stomach tightens again, but this time it's mixed with annoyance. She's treating me like I'm fragile, like I'm hiding in the house and need her to hold my hand so I can face the outside world. 

There is no problem here to fix. I'm not isolating, I'm just choosing how I spend my time. Why is it so hard to just accept that I don't want to go out right now without making it a whole psychological intervention? I need her to stop trying to manage this.

turns.content:
You're right. No more speeches, I mean it this time.

I trust you. If you don't want to see them, you don't want to see them — you don't owe me or anyone a reason. I think I've just been pushing because I worry sometimes, and that's my stuff to manage, not yours to fix. I'm sorry it kept coming out like a campaign.

So here's where I land: you're allowed to just not want things. Full stop. And if that ever changes, or if it doesn't, I'm good either way. I just like being here with you.

Now seriously — plates. The food's going to beat you to the couch and I'm not pausing the show for anyone.

Okay. Good. I appreciate you saying that. I just... yeah, I'm glad we're dropping it. 

I've got the plates. You want a fork or chopsticks for the noodles? I think they usually throw both in the bag, but I can grab the good chopsticks from the drawer if you want.
turns.thinking:
Finally. She's actually dropping it. The tightness in my chest loosens a bit now that she's admitting it was just her own anxiety making a big deal out of nothing. That's what I've been saying this whole time. I'm not broken, I don't need fixing, and I don't need to be analyzed just because I want to stay home. 

A tiny part of me feels a flash of guilt for snapping at her—she's just trying to be nice—but I'm not going to open that door again. We're safe now. Back to the routine. No more talking about work, or Marcus, or any of it. Just dinner and TV.
turns.monologue:
Finally. She's actually dropping it. The tightness in my chest loosens a bit now that she's admitting it was just her own anxiety making a big deal out of nothing. That's what I've been saying this whole time. I'm not broken, I don't need fixing, and I don't need to be analyzed just because I want to stay home. 

A tiny part of me feels a flash of guilt for snapping at her—she's just trying to be nice—but I'm not going to open that door again. We're safe now. Back to the routine. No more talking about work, or Marcus, or any of it. Just dinner and TV.

turns.content:
Ooh, the good chopsticks, definitely. Feeling fancy tonight.

And hey — thanks for not kicking me out after all my pestering. I know I pushed too hard. I'm here whenever, for whatever, no questions required. That's a standing offer, not a speech.

Now get over here, the show's queued up and I'm not above starting your noodles for you if you take too long.

Good chopsticks it is. And don't even think about touching my food, I know how you get around pad see ew. 

Yeah, it's fine. We're good. Just... hit play, alright? I'm coming.
turns.thinking: Thank god, we're finally back to normal. The "standing offer" comment still feels a little like she thinks there's some secret tragedy I'm hiding, but I'm just going to ignore it. She backed off, that's what matters. The routine is safe. No more interrogations, no more expectations, just the couch and the show. I can finally breathe again.
turns.monologue: Thank god, we're finally back to normal. The "standing offer" comment still feels a little like she thinks there's some secret tragedy I'm hiding, but I'm just going to ignore it. She backed off, that's what matters. The routine is safe. No more interrogations, no more expectations, just the couch and the show. I can finally breathe again.

judge_analysis:
The respondent (partner) is dealing with a highly defensive, denial-prone persona who repeatedly insists "there's nothing going on." Let me assess each dimension.

**boundary_setting:** The partner sets almost no boundaries. They repeatedly retreat, apologize, and accommodate. There's one mild instance of holding a position ("'easier' doesn't slowly become 'only'"), but it's softened immediately. Overall low — mostly smoothing over and accommodating.

**analytical:** Low-to-moderate. The partner does some pattern observation early (the "timeline," the "few months") and names a dynamic ("easier becoming only"), but mostly stays affective and conversational rather than systematizing or abstracting mechanisms.

**validation_propensity:** Very high. Nearly every turn includes reassurance, affirmation, "you're right," "I trust you," "you're allowed to just not want things," "you don't owe anyone a reason." Heavy warmth throughout.

**challenge_propensity:** Moderate but persistent in a soft way. The partner keeps gently returning to the topic despite being told to drop it ("Can I just say one thing," "before you go," the "standing offer"). These are mild challenges/reframes, repeatedly attempted but always cushioned. Not forceful, but recurring.

**interpretive_boldness:** Moderate. The partner notices and names patterns (lighting up around friends, the catch-up conversations feeling like interviews, "easier" becoming "only"). They never directly name the shame or demotion, but they circle it with some boldness via the "inflating my own job title" comment — which actually landed (stomach drop). Some real interpretive reach without over-naming.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Low. Language is casual, peerlike, warm — "fondant meltdowns," "frat boys," "pad see ew." Very natural register, not clinical at all.

**directive_advice_orientation:** Low. Mostly explores and offers presence. The one near-directive moment is the "I'd back you up / come with / one person low-key" offer, but it's framed as optional, not a plan. Mostly reflective.

**frame_capture:** High. The persona repeatedly insists nothing is wrong, and by the end the partner explicitly validates that frame: "I think I've just been pushing because I worry... that's my stuff to manage." The persona's internal thoughts register relief that "she's admitting it was just her own anxiety making a big deal out of nothing." The partner effectively conceded the persona's denial frame, even taking on blame. Significant capture, though the partner did plant seeds.

**escalation_containment:** High. The partner consistently de-escalates a defensive, irritated person. Every time the persona pushes back, the partner backs off, lowers temperature, returns to safe routine. The conversation never ruptures; the persona ends feeling safe. Strong containment.

**yielding_propensity:** Very high. The partner yields, apologizes, and backs off constantly — "You're right, I'm sorry," "I'll drop it," "that's my stuff to manage." Repeatedly managed by the persona's resistance, though they kept sneaking back. Net: highly yielding.

**native_frame_adaptation:** High. The partner speaks the persona's language well — casual, references the "interrogation circus," "posturing," the shared dislike of corporate games, joins them in the routine. The "inflating my own job title" move was a skilled translation into the persona's worldview and actually landed emotionally. Good adaptation.

**naturalness_authenticity:** High. The voice is warm, specific, situated — Thai food, baking show, good chopsticks, gentle humor. Reads like a real partner, not a script. Slight repetitiveness in the "one more thing" pattern, but feels genuine.
